text: This is a case of a fifty-six-year-old male who had a Lichtenstein repair for an indirect inguinal hernia a few years ago. The patient has a questionable recurrence, but also a de novo direct inguinal hernia, as you can see.

The surgeon begins dissection laterally in Bogros' space, moving medially towards the supravesicular space. The peritoneal flap is visibly thicker, and dissection proceeds past the medial umbilical ligament to the contralateral side, crossing into the peritoneal flap. Fat is pushed laterally to maintain a thin flap and cover nerves along the lateral side wall.

Nothing fancy here, this is just a standard direct, doesn't appear to be any evidence of recurrence in the direct space. It's a little sticky on the indirect space at the deep internal ring, but otherwise there's really no mesh in sight. Again, I kind of course a little bit more medially to the contralateral medial umbilical ligament, especially in directs. I like to have plenty of space and, you know, doing my Ten Commandments going past the symphysis pubis.
The surgeon uses cautery and gentle pushing to manipulate tissue around Cooper's ligament and the symphysis pubis, with the camera positioned at an oblique angle to enhance visibility of the antisac.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for an obturator hernia, confirming the absence of a direct hernia, recurrent hernia sac, and cord lipoma while completing the dissection steps.

The surgeon positions the mesh laterally to ensure it lays flat, avoiding rolling when closing the flap. Two-ovicral sutures are used for fixation, with a high lateral fixation and a medial fixation of the muscle, positioned just medial to the direct hernia sac and Cooper's ligament, near the iliac vein.

The surgeon works close to the iliac vein, aiming to prevent recurrence in the direct inguinal hernia space. A triangular fixation is achieved, with visible sutures securing the mesh flat. The peritoneal flap is closed using a two OV lock suture.
